answers.intake_q1: When did the elbow pain start?
answers.intake_q2: Can you describe where exactly on your elbow the pain is located?
answers.intake_q3: What activities or injuries, if any, do you think might have caused or worsened the pain on the outside of your elbow?
answers.intake_a3: The pain started when I was playing tennis
answers.intake_q4: Any numbness, tingling, or weakness in your arm or hand?
answers.intake_q5: Any swelling, redness, or warmth around the elbow?
answers.intake_q6: Have you tried any treatments or medications for the elbow pain, and if so, what were they and did they help?
answers.intake_a6: icing on the first day helped a little with the pain
answers.intake_q7: Any difficulty gripping or lifting objects with that arm?
answers.intake_a7: no, but I do get pain with lifting objects
answers.intake_q8: Any fever, chills, or other systemic symptoms?
answers.intake_q9: Have you noticed any improvement or worsening of the pain over the past 3 weeks?
answers.intake_summary: Patient has had outside elbow pain for 3 weeks starting after playing tennis, with pain on lifting but no numbness, swelling, or systemic symptoms. Icing helped initially but pain persists without worsening or improvement.

note.soap:
S: 
The patient is a 45-year-old female presenting with a 3-week history of pain on the outside of her elbow that began after playing tennis. She reports pain with lifting objects but denies numbness, tingling, weakness, swelling, redness, warmth, fever, or other systemic symptoms. Initial icing provided some relief, but the pain has neither improved nor worsened since onset. Patient is not pregnant.

O: 
Phone evaluation only; no physical exam or vital signs available.

A: 
The clinical presentation is consistent with lateral epicondylitis (tennis elbow), characterized by pain localized to the lateral elbow following repetitive wrist extension activities such as tennis. There is no evidence of neurological compromise or infection.

Differential: 
1. Lateral epicondylitis (tennis elbow) 
2. Radial tunnel syndrome 
3. Osteoarthritis of the elbow 
4. Referred pain from cervical radiculopathy 

P: 
 - First-line medication: Nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drugs (NSAIDs) such as ibuprofen or naproxen for pain relief. 
 - Alternatives: Acetaminophen if NSAIDs are contraindicated or not tolerated. 
 - Medication safety considerations: Use NSAIDs with caution in patients with gastrointestinal, renal, or cardiovascular risk factors; avoid in patients with known allergies to NSAIDs. Acetaminophen should be used within recommended dosing limits to avoid hepatotoxicity. 
 - Return precautions: Advise the patient to seek care if there is worsening pain, development of numbness, weakness, swelling, redness, fever, or if symptoms do not improve with conservative management over the next 2 to 4 weeks. 
 - Testing: Not indicated at this time given the typical presentation; consider imaging or referral if symptoms persist or worsen despite treatment.

evidence.title: Elbow Injuries | Elbow Disorders | MedlinePlus
evidence.snippet: bone, cartilage, ligaments and fluid. Muscles and tendons help the elbow joint move. When any of these structures is hurt or diseased, you have elbow problems. Many things can make your elbow hurt. A common cause is tendinitis , an inflammation or injury to the tendons that attach muscle to bone. Tendinitis of the elbow is a sports injury , often from playin…

evidence.title: Tendinitis: MedlinePlus
evidence.snippet: onnect muscles to bones. They help your muscles move your bones. Tendinitis is the severe swelling of a tendon. Tendinitis usually happens after repeated injury to an area such as the wrist or ankle. It causes pain and soreness around a joint. Some common forms of tendinitis are named after the sports that increase their risk. They include tennis elbow, golf…

evidence.title: Sports Injuries: MedlinePlus
evidence.snippet: ou, but sometimes you can injure yourself when you play sports or exercise. Accidents, poor training practices, or improper gear can cause them. Some people get hurt because they are not in shape. Not warming up or stretching enough can also lead to injuries. The most common sports injuries are: Sprains and strains Knee injuries Swollen muscles Achilles tend…
